steps.description: State the LHS and the plan
steps.explanation: We start from the left-hand side and aim for $\cot\theta$, rewriting the double angles as single-angle ratios so a factor cancels.

steps.description: Choose a form of cos 2theta for the numerator
steps.explanation: We pick the $2\cos^2\theta - 1$ version so that adding $1$ removes the constant and leaves a pure cosine term.
The $+1$ and $-1$ cancel, so the numerator becomes $2\cos^2\theta$.
The sine double-angle formula puts the denominator into single-angle ratios and provides the factor $2\cos\theta$ shared with the numerator.
The common factor $2\cos\theta$ is now visible in both numerator and denominator.
Dividing top and bottom by $2\cos\theta$ leaves the ratio $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}$.
By definition $\cot\theta = \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}$, which is exactly the right-hand side.
The LHS has been reduced to $\cot\theta$, matching the right-hand side.
At $\theta = 30^\circ$ both sides equal $\sqrt3$, consistent with the identity.
The LHS equals the RHS for all valid $\theta$, so the identity is proved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Using $1 + \cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2\theta$ and $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$, the fraction reduces to $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \cot\theta$, hence $\dfrac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{\sin 2\theta} \equiv \cot\theta$.
